paragraphs.body: In the essay „Ask Ethan: How can we comprehend the size of the Universe?“ by Ethan Siegel, the author tries to describe the dimensions of the universe. A challenge most of us humans, including me, really struggle with.

annotations.text: A challenge most of us humans, including me, really struggle with.
annotations.comment: Remember that summary is all "they say." There's no "I say" at this point :)

paragraphs.body: Siegel’s first big idea is the understanding of the scaling : In order to describe and comprehend the size of the universe, we have to gradually move from familiar scales, such as the human height of about two meters, to larger scales. The text describes that we are often only a few orders of magnitude away from our scale, such as from millimeters to several kilometers. This step-by-step approach helps us to better understand the dimensions by adjusting our perception and using relatable comparisons, such as the difference between the size of the earth and the distance to the sun or other stars. This makes the incomprehensible more accessible by moving a little further away from our familiar scale with each step.

Next, he explains that there are significant ratios and relationships between different scales that help us to understand the universe. For example, it shows that the size of the Earth, the distances in the solar system and the dimensions of the Milky Way are linked by similar proportions. These relationships allow us to move from one scale to the next, for example from the diameter of the Earth to the distance of the inner Oort cloud and then to the size of the Milky Way.

annotations.comment: I wonder how much of this summary is necessary to "point" the reader to your own essay and ideas? Let's talk about the "art of summary."
annotations.context_left: iameter of the Earth to the distance of the inner Oort cloud and then to the size of the Milky Way.

paragraphs.body: Siegel’s last big idea is the perspective shift: He emphasizes that it is important to change our perspective in order to understand the universe without feeling insignificant. By seeing ourselves as part of a larger cosmic context as inhabitants of Earth, members of the solar system, and components of the Milky Way. We can better locate ourselves in the vastness of the universe. The author explains that the immensity of the universe can seem overwhelming, but if we focus on the fact that everything visible is close to our cosmic “neighbor,” it becomes accessible. This perspective helps us to feel not as isolated beings but as part of an interconnected system in which the objects and structures around us play a significant role.

annotations.text: By seeing ourselves as part of a larger cosmic context as inhabitants of Earth, members of the solar system, and components of the Milky Way. We can better locate ourselves in the vastness of the universe.
annotations.comment: interesting. Let's see if this is important to your essay as a whole!

annotations.text: This perspective helps us to feel not as isolated beings but as part of an interconnected system
annotations.comment: Is "connection" an important idea for your essay?

paragraphs.body: Trying to understand the universe and how small we as humans are in that Sometimes when I sit on the train, look out of the window, and get in my thoughts I just think of myself as a person and then the people I know, the people in my town, and bigger and bigger, but at some point, it just isn’t imaginable for me anymore. That is what Ethan Siegel's article is dealing with. For all the questions I ask about this topic, he is trying to help find solutions. He tells us to do the same thing I did: breaking it into smaller steps. But he wants us to break it down on even smaller scales, because we want to reach a point we have never reached with our minds so we have to go smaller than we ever did. In one part of his text, he even talks about Nuclei and some other stuff I have only ever heard in physics before. That part of the text I don’t like that much because it doesn’t really help me get closer to understanding the dimensions of the universe.

annotations.text: Sometimes when I sit on the train, look out of the window, and get in my thoughts I just think of myself as a person and then the people I know, the people in my town,

annotations.comment: Great job shifting into your voice and perspective. This is a very compelling moment in the essay.
You're doing some interesting "reader response" to Seigel's article, but let's see if we can discover your essay's actual "center of gravity."
annotations.context_left: much because it doesn’t really help me get closer to understanding the dimensions of the universe.

paragraphs.body: The first step is to get from the size of a human to the size of the Earth, which should be pretty easy for most humans. After that is what most people struggle with the most. The Earth is one of the smaller planets. The sun for example could fit 1 million Earths in it. That is again one point where it might be very difficult for us to comprehend the size of it. But you shouldn’t really look at the size of the planets but at the distance of how far the planets move around each other. The Earth is 150 million Kilometers away from the Sun. All of a sudden, by just going up a little bit: from on Earth to above Earth’s atmosphere and by looking at the other objects in our Solar System, we’ve gone from the scale of a ~1.5-2 meter human to a ~150 billion meter orbit around our Sun. The next step is comprehending the Oort Cloud. It goes out for tens of thousands of times the Earth-Sun distance. The Milky Way is a huge collection of stars, but not infinitely large. It's about 100,000 light-years across, a distance that's comparable to the ratio of a human to Earth compared to the distance to the inner Oort Cloud. Stars are concentrated in the galactic center and along the spiral arms, while they're more sparse in other directions. Even the author as a specialist in that topic has to admit, that even with all his explanations it still is very difficult to understand for many people: “ From the scale of a human to the scale of the observable Universe seems incredibly daunting. Humans are a couple of meters in scale; the Universe that we can see extends for some 46 billion light-years in all directions.” Even I to be honest, who has read this article several times now, find myself struggling sometimes with all these different terms and thinking one step at a time. But he goes on with two tips that could help you with that problem. The first is, what I have already mentioned a couple of times in this essay and which is for me the most important, to think in reasonable steps. Another big point of the article is that you should go away from thinking out of the human perspective. You have to see it much more from a "Birds-eye view” and don’t compare your size to the size of for example planets. If you change that perspective and don’t look at it from your eyes, but from a 3rd person perspective everything is more in relation to one another. The author mentions a really good point that clarifies it even more:” f you insist on taking the leap from ourselves up to the size of the observable Universe, it’s easy to feel insignificant. But we are a part of many significant things that fit better on larger scales; we can view ourselves as creatures of Earth, members of the Solar System, a component of the Milky Way, and inhabitants of this Universe.” I think with that statement it is pretty easy to understand how “looking at it from another perspective” is meant. And to go away a little bit from the astronomic portion of the text. This mindset could be used for any other thing. For example in my soccer team. When I am not playing as much as I want to in a game and we are doing good. I often get into the mindset of” I didn’t have any part in this win” But applying Siegel’s statement, I am part of the team, a member of the offense unit, and so on and so forth. I think you can apply that to many different things and life and it will help you not only understand the universe better, but understand yourself better.

annotations.comment: I also wonder if looking from another perspective might relate to other ideas in your life as well? Let's talk :)
